content:
Hello, I finished porting my FPD-Link driver for the DS90UB954 from C to Rust! It seems to be working fine :smile:

I am interested in working towards upstreaming it. How should I go about doing that?

The driver is here.

I have dependencies on some abstractions that are currently not upstreamed yet:

  • I2C and regmap from the ncv6336 branch (Fabien Parent)
  • GPIO consumer (Fabien Parent)
  • device properties (Rob Herring)

I also had to add some abstractions myself:

  • GPIO consumer cansleep variant
    (boring, to avoid a warning)

  • msleep
    (boring. might conflict with ongoing work on delay abstractions, I haven't checked)

  • regmap changes
    The existing regmap abstractions required the usage of regmap fields, which the C driver doesn't do. In order to stay close to the C version, I removed that requirement and added simple read & write methods.

  • of: iterator over device tree nodes
    The device tree overlay for my hardware can have multiple child nodes dependening on how many serializers (DS90UB953) are connected. There is some inconsistency here, which might be avoidable. (advice welcome) I use the device property API for reading toplevel values, but the OF API for reading the nested values. Maybe I should only use one or the other? But I assume the device property isn't intended to replace the OF API completely, so maybe I need at least the OF API.

  • of: parse_phandle_with_args (extension of the above)

  • ArrayVec
    A stack-allocated growable array with a statically fixed maximum capacity. Basically a low-quality ripoff of the crate arrayvec. I believe _something like this_ could be generally useful in the kernel.
    My use case is this: The C function of_parse_phandle_with_args takes a pointer to a of_phandle_args as an out-parameter. This contains an array of args and an args_count. It would be unsafe to do this in Rust, since it allows reading from invalid memory. ArrayVec solves this problem and avoids dynamic memory allocation.

  • i2c: new_client_device
    This is easily the sloppiest abstraction I made. Returning a raw pointer to the driver code is surely no good. I just needed to be able to call i2c_new_client_device to set up an i2c client for each of the serializers connected to the main hardware. I have no idea what a "proper" abstraction could look like for this, so I didn't even try. But I'm happy to do that with some advice about designing the API in a way that might be approved by the relevant maintainers.

This is my first time contributing to the kernel, I would appreciate any form of review, including pointing out newbie mistakes, commit messages / structure etc.
Thanks!
Who is the maintainer?
The maintainer of what?
The person who would take this driver and send it to Linus?
That would be me.
Ah, cool
Sorry, I think I was unclear. I see Tomi Valkeinen in the MAINTAINERS file for the C driver.
Ahh got it :smile: So I guess they would have to be OK with accepting Rust code in the first place?
Is this a rewrite of a C driver or a new driver? I see ds90ub913.c, ds90ub953.c, ds90ub960.c, but I don't see any 954 file. I'm not sure what the significance of these numbers are.

It's a rewrite of an out-of-tree driver, so it is not prohibited from being upstreamed I think. The out-of-tree C driver is here.

The numbers are different chips. 953 is a serializer, 954 is a deserializer used in combination with the 953.
Ok, cool.
I'll let others reply to your other questions.

Remo Senekowitsch said:

Ahh got it :smile: So I guess they would have to be OK with accepting Rust code in the first place?

Yeah. The same applies other additions/changes for each of the dependencies/abstractions, i.e. each would normally go through the people responsible for it.

Remo Senekowitsch said:

of: iterator over device tree nodes
The device tree overlay for my hardware can have multiple child nodes dependening on how many serializers (DS90UB953) are connected. There is some inconsistency here, which might be avoidable. (advice welcome) I use the device property API for reading toplevel values, but the OF API for reading the nested values. Maybe I should only use one or the other? But I assume the device property isn't intended to replace the OF API completely, so maybe I need at least the OF API.

Regarding the device property API vs. OF API:
Rob Herring said:

it's really the device_property_* API that we should be making rust bindings for, not the DT API

As far as I understood the topic are pointers pointing directly into the device tree which might change (e.g. due to overlays).

Regarding the concrete commit from you I will have a look to it and note directly in the code at github if I have any comments :wink:

Thank for the input. I replied to your comment on GitHub by mail, for some reason I can't see them in the UI. I will switch to using the device property API, now that I found out it can also iterate over child nodes.

I have to figure out how to handle the relationship between device and fwnode_handle... device_get_named_child_node returns a fwnode_handle pointer and device_get_next_child_node as well. The property reading functions are duplicated for these two types on the C side. Would be nice if that wasn't necessary in Rust. If I create an iterator over fwnode_handle (or a wrapper thereof) for accessing children, I need some additional mechanism to give the user access to these functions. fwnode_handle holds a pointer to its device, maybe I could use that? But the definition of fwnode_handle warns against using it.

Any thoughts?

Remo Senekowitsch said:

Any thoughts?

What do you think about [1]? This goes on top of @Rob Herring's version. This is just an idea, so sorry if it is total non-sense :wink: I haven't tried it, just built it :innocent:

The idea from your description above is that we need a fwnode_handle to iterate over the children. And, thinking from the end, what we want is to read a property. Whether it is in the root node or in a child doesn't really matter. In the end we just want the value. So the idea is to drop Rob's newly introduced device_property_read_int_array() and instead use fwnode_property_read_int_array(). What, as you state, operates on fwnode_handle (we need to make it available from the C side and add a dev_fwnode() helper). With that, we are ready to work with fwnode_handles. Next step is to pass an optional childname. If childname is None we simply read from the root node as we have done before with device_property_read_int_array(). But if it's not None we search for the child node, get it's fwnode_handle an pass this instead of the root one for reading the property (from the child, then).

[1]

 drivers/base/property.c              | 14 ++---------
 include/linux/property.h             |  6 +++--
 rust/helpers/device.c                |  6 +++++
 rust/kernel/device.rs                | 37 +++++++++++++++++++++-------
 samples/rust/rust_driver_platform.rs |  2 +-
 5 files changed, 41 insertions(+), 24 deletions(-)

diff --git a/drivers/base/property.c b/drivers/base/property.c
index 4ff4bedaf6302..d7c8573403f43 100644
--- a/drivers/base/property.c
+++ b/drivers/base/property.c
@@ -254,7 +254,7 @@ int device_property_match_string(const struct device *dev, const char *propname,
 }
 EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL(device_property_match_string);

-static int fwnode_property_read_int_array(const struct fwnode_handle *fwnode,
+int fwnode_property_read_int_array(const struct fwnode_handle *fwnode,
                      const char *propname,
                      unsigned int elem_size, void *val,
                      size_t nval)
@@ -272,17 +272,7 @@ static int fwnode_property_read_int_array(const struct fwnode_handle *fwnode,
    return fwnode_call_int_op(fwnode->secondary, property_read_int_array, propname,
                  elem_size, val, nval);
 }
-
-/* For internal use by rust */
-int device_property_read_int_array(const struct device *dev,
-                  const char *propname,
-                  unsigned int elem_size, void *val,
-                  size_t nval)
-{
-   return fwnode_property_read_int_array(dev_fwnode(dev), propname, elem_size,
-                         val, nval);
-}
-EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL(device_property_read_int_array);
+EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL(fwnode_property_read_int_array);

 /**
  * fwnode_property_read_u8_array - return a u8 array property of firmware node
diff --git a/include/linux/property.h b/include/linux/property.h
index 99294312ad13c..40f9484a8a47f 100644
--- a/include/linux/property.h
+++ b/include/linux/property.h
@@ -45,8 +45,6 @@ int device_property_read_u32_array(const struct device *dev, const char *propnam
                   u32 *val, size_t nval);
 int device_property_read_u64_array(const struct device *dev, const char *propname,
                   u64 *val, size_t nval);
-int device_property_read_int_array(const struct device *dev, const char *propname,
-                  unsigned int elem_size, void *val, size_t nval);
 int device_property_read_string_array(const struct device *dev, const char *propname,
                      const char **val, size_t nval);
 int device_property_read_string(const struct device *dev, const char *propname,
@@ -68,6 +66,10 @@ int fwnode_property_read_u32_array(const struct fwnode_handle *fwnode,
 int fwnode_property_read_u64_array(const struct fwnode_handle *fwnode,
                   const char *propname, u64 *val,
                   size_t nval);
+int fwnode_property_read_int_array(const struct fwnode_handle *fwnode,
+                  const char *propname,
+                  unsigned int elem_size, void *val,
+                  size_t nval);
 int fwnode_property_read_string_array(const struct fwnode_handle *fwnode,
                      const char *propname, const char **val,
                      size_t nval);
diff --git a/rust/helpers/device.c b/rust/helpers/device.c
index b2135c6686b02..08dfd04953e1a 100644
--- a/rust/helpers/device.c
+++ b/rust/helpers/device.c
@@ -1,6 +1,7 @@
 // SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-2.0

 #include <linux/device.h>
+#include <linux/property.h>

 int rust_helper_devm_add_action(struct device *dev,
                void (*action)(void *),
@@ -8,3 +9,8 @@ int rust_helper_devm_add_action(struct device *dev,
 {
    return devm_add_action(dev, action, data);
 }
+
+struct fwnode_handle *rust_helper_dev_fwnode(struct device *dev)
+{
+   return dev_fwnode(dev);
+}
diff --git a/rust/kernel/device.rs b/rust/kernel/device.rs
index 8106801592dfc..46389058f8411 100644
--- a/rust/kernel/device.rs
+++ b/rust/kernel/device.rs
@@ -12,6 +12,7 @@
     str::{CStr, CString},
     types::{ARef, Integer, Opaque},
 };
+use crate::bindings::dev_fwnode;
 use core::{ffi::c_void, fmt, mem::MaybeUninit, ptr};

 #[cfg(CONFIG_PRINTK)]
@@ -240,7 +241,7 @@ pub fn property_match_string(&self, name: &CStr, match_str: &CStr) -> Result<usi
     pub fn property_read<T: Integer>(&self, name: &CStr, default: Option<T>) -> Result<T> {
         let default = default.map(|default| [default; 1]);

-        let val = Self::property_read_array(self, name, default)?;
+        let val = Self::property_read_array(self, name, default, None)?;
         Ok(val[0])
     }

@@ -249,14 +250,32 @@ pub fn property_read_array<T: Integer, const N: usize>(
         &self,
         name: &CStr,
         default: Option<[T; N]>,
+        childname: Option<&CStr>,
     ) -> Result<[T; N]> {
         let val: [MaybeUninit<T>; N] = [const { MaybeUninit::uninit() }; N];

-        // SAFETY: `name` is non-null and null-terminated. `self.as_raw` is valid
-        // because `self` is valid. `val.as_ptr` is valid because `val` is valid.
+        // SAFETY: self.as_raw` is valid because `self` is valid.
+        let mut fwnode = unsafe { dev_fwnode(self.as_raw())};
+
+        match childname {
+            Some(childname) => {
+                // SAFETY: `childname` is non-null and null-terminated.
+                // `fwnode` is valid as it is obtained from `self`.
+                fwnode = unsafe {
+                    bindings::fwnode_get_named_child_node(
+                        fwnode,
+                        childname.as_char_ptr(),
+                    )
+                }
+            }
+            None => (),
+        }
+
+        // SAFETY: `name` is non-null and null-terminated.
+        // `val.as_ptr` is valid because `val` is valid.
         let ret = unsafe {
-            bindings::device_property_read_int_array(
-                self.as_raw(),
+            bindings::fwnode_property_read_int_array(
+                fwnode,
                 name.as_ptr() as *const i8,
                 T::SIZE as u32,
                 val.as_ptr() as *mut c_void,
@@ -290,8 +309,8 @@ pub fn property_read_array_vec<T: Integer>(&self, name: &CStr, len: usize) -> Re
         // SAFETY: `name` is non-null and null-terminated. `self.as_raw` is valid
         // because `self` is valid. `val.as_ptr` is valid because `val` is valid.
         to_result(unsafe {
-            bindings::device_property_read_int_array(
-                self.as_raw(),
+            bindings::fwnode_property_read_int_array(
+                dev_fwnode(self.as_raw()),
                 name.as_ptr() as *const i8,
                 T::SIZE as u32,
                 val.as_ptr() as *mut c_void,
@@ -310,8 +329,8 @@ pub fn property_count_elem<T: Integer>(&self, name: &CStr) -> Result<usize> {
         // because `self` is valid. Passing null pointer buffer is valid to obtain
         // the number of elements in the property array.
         let ret = unsafe {
-            bindings::device_property_read_int_array(
-                self.as_raw(),
+            bindings::fwnode_property_read_int_array(
+                dev_fwnode(self.as_raw()),
                 name.as_ptr() as *const i8,
                 T::SIZE as u32,
                 ptr::null_mut(),
diff --git a/samples/rust/rust_driver_platform.rs b/samples/rust/rust_driver_platform.rs
index 48fd6f4733b45..f457f8062322c 100644
--- a/samples/rust/rust_driver_platform.rs
+++ b/samples/rust/rust_driver_platform.rs
@@ -61,7 +61,7 @@ fn probe(pdev: &mut platform::Device, info: Option<&Self::IdInfo>) -> Result<Pin
         let prop: u32 = dev.property_read(c_str!("test,u32-prop"), None)?;
         dev_info!(dev, "'test,u32-prop' is {:#x}\n", prop);

-        let prop: [i16; 4] = dev.property_read_array(c_str!("test,i16-array"), None)?;
+        let prop: [i16; 4] = dev.property_read_array(c_str!("test,i16-array"), None, None)?;
         dev_info!(dev, "'test,i16-array' is {:?}\n", prop);
         dev_info!(
             dev,
--
2.48.0

I might be misunderstanding something, but I'm not sure if this is flexible enough. For example, this doesn't allow traversing the device node tree to an arbitrary depth, right? My dt overlay looks something like this:

fpdlink: ds90ub954@30 {
    // ...
    serializers {
        // ...
        serializer@0 { /* ... */ }
        serializer@1 { /* ... */ }
    }
}

With the above approach, I don't see how I can read properties from "grandchildren", i.e. serializer@0 and serializer@1.

However, I didn't know about dev_fwnode() and this is giving me another idea. We could create a FwNode wrapper around fwnode_handle and implement the device property APIs only for that. Then we add impl AsRef<FwNode> for Device {} where we call dev_fwnode(). That way a Rust driver author can just read properties and iterate over children freely, without worrying about Device vs FwNode. Also, we don't have to duplicate the API on the Rust side.

Can dev_fwnode() fail if the device is non-null? At least I see many functions that call dev_fwnode(dev) directly without any null checks. Otherwise we couldn't use AsRef, but a custom trait with a fallible conversion signature wouldn't be much worse. Usage could look something like:

dev.as_fwnode()?.read_property()

As I'm scrolling through property.c, I think this actually matches the approach C takes pretty closely. All the device_* functions I can see don't do much more than call the corresponding fwnode_* function on dev_fwnode(dev). I will try to implement a PoC and report back with the results :smile:

Dirk Behme said:

Remo Senekowitsch said:

Any thoughts?

What do you think about [1]? This goes on top of Rob Herring's version. This is just an idea, so sorry if it is total non-sense :wink: I haven't tried it, just built it :innocent:

The idea from your description above is that we need a fwnode_handle to iterate over the children. And, thinking from the end, what we want is to read a property. Whether it is in the root node or in a child doesn't really matter. In the end we just want the value. So the idea is to drop Rob's newly introduced device_property_read_int_array() and instead use fwnode_property_read_int_array(). What, as you state, operates on fwnode_handle (we need to make it available from the C side and add a dev_fwnode() helper). With that, we are ready to work with fwnode_handles. Next step is to pass an optional childname. If childname is None we simply read from the root node as we have done before with device_property_read_int_array(). But if it's not None we search for the child node, get it's fwnode_handle an pass this instead of the root one for reading the property (from the child, then).

No, no, no! You are mixing reading and parsing a property with looking for child node. Keep traversing nodes (refcounted) and reading properties (not refcounted) separate.

The preference in C drivers is use device_property_ over fwnode_property_ over of_property_. (Personally, I don't really care for the whole fwnode abstraction because it came out of the "let's use DT bindings in ACPI" effort which I think is flawed. But that's besides the point now.) I started with device_property_ being the preference, but that's only ever going to work for simple cases which only need to read properties from the node associated with the struct device.

Perhaps the property functions should be a trait which can be implemented for Device, some struct FwNode, etc. Then whether we do dev.property_read() or fwnode.property_read() doesn't matter too much. But can the Device implementation just call the FwNode implementation? I would think so...

Remo Senekowitsch said:

I might be misunderstanding something, but I'm not sure if this is flexible enough. For example, this doesn't allow traversing the device node tree to an arbitrary depth, right? My dt overlay looks something like this:

fpdlink: ds90ub954@30 {
    // ...
    serializers {
        // ...
        serializer@0 { /* ... */ }
        serializer@1 { /* ... */ }
    }
}

With the above approach, I don't see how I can read properties from "grandchildren", i.e. serializer@0 and serializer@1.

However, I didn't know about dev_fwnode() and this is giving me another idea. We could create a FwNode wrapper around fwnode_handle and implement the device property APIs only for that. Then we add impl AsRef<FwNode> for Device {} where we call dev_fwnode(). That way a Rust driver author can just read properties and iterate over children freely, without worrying about Device vs FwNode. Also, we don't have to duplicate the API on the Rust side.

Can dev_fwnode() fail if the device is non-null? At least I see many functions that call dev_fwnode(dev) directly without any null checks. Otherwise we couldn't use AsRef, but a custom trait with a fallible conversion signature wouldn't be much worse. Usage could look something like:

dev.as_fwnode()?.read_property()

As I'm scrolling through property.c, I think this actually matches the approach C takes pretty closely. All the device_* functions I can see don't do much more than call the corresponding fwnode_* function on dev_fwnode(dev). I will try to implement a PoC and report back with the results :smile:

I guess this similar to what I was suggesting. dev_fwnode() can be NULL, but generally we know it is not. If a driver instance can only be instantiated from DT or ACPI, then fwnode can't be NULL. The only other way is the kernel creating devices (some drivers create sub-devices) and then fwnode can be NULL. But we wouldn't be reading properties in that case.

Alright, I've got something that compiles. I can't quite test it yet, because I don't know what the equivalent of of_parse_phandle_with_args is in the device property API. Gotta figure that out next.

This patch defines FwNode, moves all property read functions over to it and allows easy conversion from &Device to &FwNode via AsRef using dev_fwnode().

And this patch allows accessing children and iterating over them at arbitrary depth. The implementation looks essentially the same as the one based on the OF API.

@Remo Senekowitsch It's fwnode_property_get_reference_args you are looking for.

The patches look alright. My main comment is that I think much of it needs to move out to fwnode.rs or something. How will reading a property in a child node iterator work without an associated Device struct for example.

Rob Herring said:

it needs to move out to fwnode.rs or something.

Yes, let's move it. What's about property.rs? Same file name like in C.

I've put it in fwnode.rs for now, let me know if I should move it again. property.rs makes sense too.

patches:

  • moving property stuff to fwnode.rs
  • accessor and iterator over child nodes
  • fwnode_property_get_reference_args
I could also test this with my driver now, seems to work fine.

Remo Senekowitsch said:

I could also test this with my driver now, seems to work fine.

You might want to enable Kernel hacking > Sample kernel code > Rust samples -> Platform Driver what will give a nice impression of the before & after (for example like [1]). With that, two comments:

First, we should try to avoid the unwrap() as its supposed to be heavily discouraged.

Second, in your usage example/driver you "hide" the dev.as_fwnode().unwrap() behind a helper variable let fwnode = dev.as_fwnode().unwrap(); . It might be personal taste, but I wonder if we could make the API somehow more intuitive? And not exposing the fwnode() and unwrap() in the API? Not sure if anything more intuitive like e.g.

dev.property().read_string();

dev.property().present();

etc. would be possible?

Besides these details, many thanks for working on this topic! :grinning:

[1]

Subject: [PATCH] samples: rust_driver_platform: Switch to new property API

Signed-off-by: Dirk Behme <dirk.behme@de.bosch.com>
---
 samples/rust/rust_driver_platform.rs | 20 ++++++++++----------
 1 file changed, 10 insertions(+), 10 deletions(-)

diff --git a/samples/rust/rust_driver_platform.rs b/samples/rust/rust_driver_platform.rs
index e6b9e1ecab4d5..66ae049e00e9f 100644
--- a/samples/rust/rust_driver_platform.rs
+++ b/samples/rust/rust_driver_platform.rs
@@ -29,24 +29,24 @@ fn probe(pdev: &mut platform::Device, info: Option<&Self::IdInfo>) -> Result<Pin
         }

         let dev = pdev.as_ref();
-        if let Ok(idx) = dev.property_match_string(c_str!("compatible"), c_str!("test,rust-device"))
+        if let Ok(idx) = dev.as_fwnode().unwrap().property_match_string(c_str!("compatible"), c_str!("test,rust-device"))
         {
             dev_info!(pdev.as_ref(), "matched compatible string idx = {}\n", idx);
         }

-        if let Ok(str) = dev.property_read_string(c_str!("compatible"))
+        if let Ok(str) = dev.as_fwnode().unwrap().property_read_string(c_str!("compatible"))
         {
             dev_info!(pdev.as_ref(), "compatible string = {:?}\n", str);
         }

-        let prop = dev.property_read_bool(c_str!("test,bool-prop"));
+        let prop = dev.as_fwnode().unwrap().property_read_bool(c_str!("test,bool-prop"));
         dev_info!(dev, "bool prop is {}\n", prop);

-        if dev.property_present(c_str!("test,u32-prop")) {
+        if dev.as_fwnode().unwrap().property_present(c_str!("test,u32-prop")) {
             dev_info!(dev, "'test,u32-prop' is present\n");
         }

-        let prop = dev.property_read::<u32>(c_str!("test,u32-optional-prop"), Some(0x12))?;
+        let prop = dev.as_fwnode().unwrap().property_read::<u32>(c_str!("test,u32-optional-prop"), Some(0x12))?;
         dev_info!(
             dev,
             "'test,u32-optional-prop' is {:#x} (default = {:#x})\n",
@@ -55,21 +55,21 @@ fn probe(pdev: &mut platform::Device, info: Option<&Self::IdInfo>) -> Result<Pin
         );

         // Missing property without a default will print an error
-        let _ = dev.property_read::<u32>(c_str!("test,u32-required-prop"), None);
+        let _ = dev.as_fwnode().unwrap().property_read::<u32>(c_str!("test,u32-required-prop"), None);

-        let prop: u32 = dev.property_read(c_str!("test,u32-prop"), None)?;
+        let prop: u32 = dev.as_fwnode().unwrap().property_read(c_str!("test,u32-prop"), None)?;
         dev_info!(dev, "'test,u32-prop' is {:#x}\n", prop);

-        let prop: [i16; 4] = dev.property_read_array(c_str!("test,i16-array"), None)?;
+        let prop: [i16; 4] = dev.as_fwnode().unwrap().property_read_array(c_str!("test,i16-array"), None)?;
         dev_info!(dev, "'test,i16-array' is {:?}\n", prop);
         dev_info!(
             dev,
             "'test,i16-array' length is {}\n",
-            dev.property_count_elem::<u16>(c_str!("test,i16-array"))
+            dev.as_fwnode().unwrap().property_count_elem::<u16>(c_str!("test,i16-array"))
                 .unwrap()
         );

-        let prop: KVec<i16> = dev.property_read_array_vec(c_str!("test,i16-array"), 4)?;
+        let prop: KVec<i16> = dev.as_fwnode().unwrap().property_read_array_vec(c_str!("test,i16-array"), 4)?;
         dev_info!(dev, "'test,i16-array' is KVec {:?}\n", prop);

         let drvdata = KBox::new(Self { pdev: pdev.clone() }, GFP_KERNEL)?;
--
2.48.0

Dirk Behme said:

First, we should try to avoid the unwrap()

Would this [1] be an option for this? It keeps the panic for the unlikely case that dev_fwnode() is NULL. But it removes that check from the API.

[1]

Subject: [PATCH] rust: fwnode: Move the fwnode NULL check to the helper

In [1] Rob Herring explains:

"dev_fwnode() can be NULL, but generally we know it is not.
If a driver instance can only be instantiated from DT or ACPI,
then fwnode can't be NULL. The only other way is the kernel
creating devices (some drivers create sub-devices) and then
fwnode can be NULL. But we wouldn't be reading properties in
that case."

The C side is using dev_fwnode() *without* checking it for
being NULL. However, to be on the safe side, let's do it.
As both, the explanation from Rob Herring and the C side
usage assume that dev_fwnode() is not NULL, it is justified
to call panic() in case it is.

Link: https://rust-for-linux.zulipchat.com/#narrow/channel/288089-General/topic/DS90UB954.20driver.20done.2C.20ready.20to.20upstream.3F/near/495790434 [1]
Signed-off-by: Dirk Behme <dirk.behme@de.bosch.com>
---
 rust/helpers/device.c                |  7 ++++++-
 rust/kernel/device.rs                | 15 +++++----------
 samples/rust/rust_driver_platform.rs | 20 ++++++++++----------
 3 files changed, 21 insertions(+), 21 deletions(-)

diff --git a/rust/helpers/device.c b/rust/helpers/device.c
index 08dfd04953e1a..2118c25992330 100644
--- a/rust/helpers/device.c
+++ b/rust/helpers/device.c
@@ -12,5 +12,10 @@ int rust_helper_devm_add_action(struct device *dev,

 struct fwnode_handle *rust_helper_dev_fwnode(struct device *dev)
 {
-   return dev_fwnode(dev);
+   struct fwnode_handle *fwnode = dev_fwnode(dev);
+
+   if (unlikely(!fwnode))
+       panic("Device %s has no fwnode\n", dev_name(dev));
+
+   return fwnode;
 }
diff --git a/rust/kernel/device.rs b/rust/kernel/device.rs
index fbb19f8c09bab..983b89b692f7a 100644
--- a/rust/kernel/device.rs
+++ b/rust/kernel/device.rs
@@ -183,16 +183,11 @@ unsafe fn printk(&self, klevel: &[u8], msg: fmt::Arguments<'_>) {
     }

     /// Optain the fwnode corresponding to the device.
-    pub fn as_fwnode(&self) -> Option<&FwNode> {
-        // SAFETY: `self` is valid.
-        let fwnode_handle = unsafe { bindings::dev_fwnode(self.as_raw()) };
-        if fwnode_handle.is_null() {
-            return None;
-        }
-        // SAFETY: `fwnode_handle` is valid. Its lifetime is tied to `&self`. We
-        // return a reference instead of an `ARef<FwNode>` because `dev_fwnode()`
-        // doesn't increment the refcount.
-        Some(unsafe { &*fwnode_handle.cast() })
+    pub fn as_fwnode(&self) -> &FwNode {
+        // SAFETY: `self` is valid. The returned `fwnode_handle` is valid. Its
+        // lifetime is tied to `&self`. We return a reference instead of an
+        // `ARef<FwNode>` because `dev_fwnode()` doesn't increment the refcount.
+        unsafe { &*bindings::dev_fwnode(self.as_raw()).cast() }
     }
 }

(I haven't read the discussion -- just talking purely from seeing the panic in the helper :)

 };

     }

     /// Optain the fwnode corresponding to the device.

         // SAFETY: `self` is valid.
         let fwnode_handle = unsafe { bindings::dev_fwnode(self.as_raw()) };
         if fwnode_handle.is_null() {

         // return a reference instead of an `ARef<FwNode>` because `dev_fwnode()`
         // doesn't increment the refcount.

Recall that if the methods are defined on the trait, then the trait itself must be in scope for the methods to be available:

use crate::property::AccessProperty; // or whatever the name would be

fwnode.property_read_string();

Also, if we make Device::as_fwnode() infallible - which seems reasonable to me - then I can go back to making it an implementation of the AsRef trait, which will enable writing generic code over Device and FwNode anyway.

Rob Herring said:

dev_fwnode() can be NULL, but generally we know it is not. If a driver instance can only be instantiated from DT or ACPI, then fwnode can't be NULL. The only other way is the kernel creating devices (some drivers create sub-devices) and then fwnode can be NULL. But we wouldn't be reading properties in that case.

@Rob Herring What's the reason for the default argument in property_read<T: Integer> and property_read_array? It seems to me that if a default is provided, the functions cannot fail anymore. But the user still has to do error handling, which might be confusing. Why not property_read("name").unwrap_or(default_value)?

Remo Senekowitsch said:

Rob Herring What's the reason for the default argument in property_read<T: Integer> and property_read_array? It seems to me that if a default is provided, the functions cannot fail anymore. But the user still has to do error handling, which might be confusing. Why not property_read("name").unwrap_or(default_value)?

Primarily so the method can be used as an initializer. If there's a better way to do that, then that's fine. Also, we have either required or optional properties. If the property is missing and required, then we may want to print an error msg (in the core, not every caller). If there's a default, then the property is implicitly optional.

If the property is missing and required, then we may want to print an error msg (in the core, not every caller). If there's a default, then the property is implicitly optional.

Is it unrealistic to have an optional property where if it doesn't exist, I don't want to do anything? In that case I wouldn't provide a default value, but I also wouldn't want an error to be logged by core.

It would be possible to have an API that looks something like this:

property_read("name").required()?;
property_read("name").optional()?;
property_read("name").with_default(val);

It forces the user to specify the intent, allowing us to log an error appropriately (only when required is used). Might be overkill though, just an idea.

That sounds like a pretty convoluted API to me. I have to pass a default value to suppress an error message I don't want... so I have to find a sentinel value that's invalid for my use case and pass that... then I have to do error handling even though I know the function can't fail... lastly I have to check for the sentinel value and not do anything in that case. That doesn't spark joy.

I just wanted to explain some understanding/background. This shouldn't have been an argument/discussion about good/bad API :wink:

With that, again just for understanding: Would there be a behavioral difference between property_read("name").optional()?; and property_read("name").with_default("some out of range value");? I mean, if the first would require to check for the returned error, the second for the returned "out of range value", the end result would be the same, then? We would need to be careful that the API users won't become confused what to use. Or use both if both options are possible.

the first would require to check for the returned error, the second for the returned "out of range value", the end result would be the same, then?

Yeah, it would still be possible to abuse the with_default function for the same purpose as optional, but I'm not sure why people would accidentally do that. Here's what the usage would look like with a little more context:

// good
if let Ok(value) = dev.property_read().optional() {
    // property present, do special stuff
}

// bad
let value = dev.property_read().with_default("sentinel");
if value != "sentinel" {
    // property present, do special stuff
}

The regular Option type in Rust can essentially be abused in the same way, but I don't think there is a wide spread problem of people doing that.

The container type with these three methods would definitely have some documentation about when to use which.

Thanks! :+1:

The past days I was thinking about what we discussed above as well. So before answering specifically to your topics above, some general thoughts (ideas? proposals?):

  • Meanwhile, we have property_present() in mainline. So when we shuffle something around (fwnode.rs, or property.rs, see below) we need to remember that.
  • I would vote for not adding any property_*() related changes to device.rs. If required, add an additional impl device {} into our fwnode.rs (or property.rs, see below).
  • I would propose property.rs instead of fwnode.rs. Like on the C side. This can contain both the dev.property_*() and the fwnode.property_*() then. Like on the C side.
  • Having both, the dev.property_*() and the fwnode.property_*() would allow us to keep as_fwnode() private. Depending on what the user has, a device node or a fwnode, they can call either dev.property_*() or fwnode.property_*(). I find that more ergonomic than asking the user to use as_fwnode(). Hide that from the user.
  • Yes, having both implies some kind of duplication. But that could be kept thin as the device part just does the (private!) as_fwnode() to call the fwnode part which does the real work. An example just for "moving" the existing property_present() out of device.rs into property.rs:
// File: property.rs

/// A trait for device properties.
pub trait Property {
    /// Checks if property is present or not.
    fn property_present(&self, name: &CStr) -> bool;
}

impl Device {
    /// Optain the fwnode corresponding to the device.
    /// Note: NOT pub!
    fn as_fwnode(&self) -> &FwNode {
       // ...
    }
}

impl Property for Device {
    /// Checks if property is present or not.
    fn property_present(&self, name: &CStr) -> bool {
        self.as_fwnode().property_present(name)
    }
}

#[repr(transparent)]
pub struct FwNode(Opaque<bindings::fwnode_handle>);

impl FwNode {
    /// Returns the raw pointer to the `struct fwnode_handle`.
    pub fn as_raw(&self) -> *mut bindings::fwnode_handle {
        self.0.get()
    }
}

impl Property for FwNode {
    /// Returns if a firmware property `name` is present
    fn property_present(&self, name: &CStr) -> bool {
        // SAFETY: `name` is non-null and null-terminated. `self.as_raw` is valid
        // because `self` is valid.
        unsafe { bindings::fwnode_property_present(self.as_raw().cast_const(), name.as_char_ptr()) }
    }
}

This is intended as example, only. It needs to be extended to all other property_*() we have.

@Remo Senekowitsch How's it going with this? I've got some time now to get back to rust things if you need any help.

Thanks! No progress on the code, I was a little busy. But the design keeps bubbling up in the back of my mind :smile: There are still a couple points I'm not 100% happy with. The logging and the requirement to import a trait for users... I think I want to implement the current idea as is and also try another approach so we can compare them.

I'm thinking about a struct that stores both a reference to the device and a fwnode. That way we can read children via fwnode and do the auto-logging associated to the device consistently. But I heaven't thought through all the details yet, hopefully I'll have code to share soon.

Another idea I keep thinking about is this: There are concrete methods property_read_string, but also a generic method property_read<T: Integer>. What if there was only one method, generic over a trait that's implemented by all types of properties? This might be very ergonomic combined with type inference.

How I'm envisioning the usage:

// one-off reading
let foo: CString = dev.properties().read("some-string").required()?;

// reading multiple things
let props = dev.properties();
let bar: i32 = props.read("some-i32").or(0);
for child in props.children() {
    let baz: u64 = child.read("some-u64").required()?;
}

Importantly, props and child in the above have the same concrete type (removing the need to import a trait over device and fwnode). Reading from child also logs automatically associated with the device.

Thoughts? (Feel free to wait for the code before giving feedback, in case this rambling doesn't make sense without it :smile:)

It can be handled by dev.property(). Maybe that's already considered an undesirable "init" function, compared with a more direct dev.property_read(). But I think I like the former, it has a slight DX advantage where auto-complete for the methods of a device will not be cluttered with all property-related methods. Once you type dev.property(), you only get the preoperty-related methods.

Dirk Behme said:

And at struct usage time each time we have to check if e.g. the device reference is there (because we might not have it)?

I think we should always know that we have the device, no checking needed. Some pseudo code to illustrate what I'm imagining:

struct PropertyNode<'a> {
    dev: &'a Device,
    fwnode: &'a FwNode,
}

impl Device {
    fn property(&self) -> PropertyNode {
        PropertyNode {
            dev: self,
            fwnode: self.as_fwnode(),
        }
    }
}

Lifetimes on structs have some risk of becoming unergonomic, in which case an owned refcount might be considered. But I don't think it'll be necessary, users are unlikely to store such a property node for longer than the compiler can trivially infer the reference to be valid.

Reading child nodes would then always yield a new PropertyNode with a different fwnode but the same dev, enabling consistent logging.

Remo Senekowitsch said:

I think we should always know that we have the device, no checking needed. Some pseudo code to illustrate what I'm imagining:

struct PropertyNode<'a> {
    dev: &'a Device,
    fwnode: &'a FwNode,
}

impl Device {
    fn property(&self) -> PropertyNode {
        PropertyNode {
            dev: self,
            fwnode: self.as_fwnode(),
        }
    }
}

Lifetimes on structs have some risk of becoming unergonomic, in which case an owned refcount might be considered. But I don't think it'll be necessary, users are unlikely to store such a property node for longer than the compiler can trivially infer the reference to be valid.

How does this work when you don't have a Device?

What's a PropertyNode? I have nodes and I have properties. Those are separate things. Besides the naming, I don't see why we need a 2nd way to associate a fwnode with a Device.

The primary reason we have device_property_*() functions in C is just to avoid exposing the fwnode to the caller. It's really a minimal gain and maybe not something we keep on the rust side. If we need to expose something to the caller anyways for rust, then it should just be the fwnode rather than yet another construct.

How does this work when you don't have a Device?

It doesn't. I didn't know it's possible to have a fwnode without an associated device.

I don't see why we need a 2nd way to associate a fwnode with a Device.

If we need to expose something to the caller anyways for rust, then it should just be the fwnode rather than yet another construct.

The reason for this additional construct is to be able to emit logs associated with the device in the core:

If the property is missing and required, then we may want to print an error msg (in the core, not every caller).

Remo Senekowitsch said:

property-separate-file

Starting with the first one :grinning:

First, many thanks for introducing rust/kernel/property.rs. Ack :+1: I was unclear why that patch doesn't change the user of property_present(). But I think we don't have one, yet? So no user to be changed :+1:

Second, I was under the impression that we want to go with both, fwnode and device? And with this having fwnode non public? I'm referring to this part of above discussion (copying the example from above here [1]).

[1]

// File: property.rs

/// A trait for device properties.
pub trait Property {
    /// Checks if property is present or not.
    fn property_present(&self, name: &CStr) -> bool;
}

impl Device {
    /// Optain the fwnode corresponding to the device.
    /// Note: NOT pub!
    fn as_fwnode(&self) -> &FwNode {
       // ...
    }
}

impl Property for Device {
    /// Checks if property is present or not.
    fn property_present(&self, name: &CStr) -> bool {
        self.as_fwnode().property_present(name)
    }
}

#[repr(transparent)]
pub struct FwNode(Opaque<bindings::fwnode_handle>);

impl FwNode {
    /// Returns the raw pointer to the `struct fwnode_handle`.
    pub fn as_raw(&self) -> *mut bindings::fwnode_handle {
        self.0.get()
    }
}

impl Property for FwNode {
    /// Returns if a firmware property `name` is present
    fn property_present(&self, name: &CStr) -> bool {
        // SAFETY: `name` is non-null and null-terminated. `self.as_raw` is valid
        // because `self` is valid.
        unsafe { bindings::fwnode_property_present(self.as_raw().cast_const(), name.as_char_ptr()) }
    }
}

The list of people to CC seems a little excessive

It doesn't seem too long -- half of it is just because of Rust, but the main people are above.

Normally I would recommend checking manually MAINTAINERS to double-check the subsystems that you should be sending it to etc.

Are the lists supposed to go in the TO field and the individuals in CC ?

I always suggest using To for those that really need to see the email (e.g. maintainers), and Cc everyone else (e.g. lists). People may have different filters for Cc.
